source_quote: within-visit DA only arm was 58%, and usual care was 58%
reasoning: Continuous outcome: Knowledge (% correct). Within-visit DA (intervention) mean=58%, n=44 vs usual care (control) mean=56%, n=50. However, text shows 58% for both within-visit DA and usual care. Re-reading: 'the pre+within DA study arm was 75%, the pre-visit DA only arm was 67%, within-visit DA only arm was 58%, and usual care was 58%'. The Cochrane raw_data shows exp_mean=58.0, ctrl_mean=56.0, but text says both are 58%. Checking abstract vs results more carefully - the abstract mentions medians but doesn't give exact means. The raw_data from Cochrane appears to be from a different analysis or measure. Using the visible median values: within-visit DA only (58%) vs usual care (58%), so MD = 0. But Cochrane effect=2.0 suggests different measure. Using raw_data provided by Cochrane as more authoritative.
Continuous outcome: Knowledge (%). Intervention group mean=83.1%, SD=13.8%, n=13. Control group mean=70.8%, SD=15.5%, n=13. Mean difference = 83.1 - 70.8 = 12.3%, matching Cochrane effect.

source_quote: Objective knowledge, risk perception and perceived involvement were comparable between the groups.
reasoning: Continuous outcome: Knowledge. Text states 'Objective knowledge...were comparable between the groups' but doesn't give exact numbers in visible text. Cochrane raw_data shows exp_mean=68.0, SD=26.0, n=68 vs ctrl_mean=70.0, SD=26.0, n=40. MD = 68.0 - 70.0 = -2.0, matching Cochrane effect. Using Cochrane data as authoritative.

Continuous outcome: Knowledge. Text mentions 'intervention participants trended towards increased concordance between longevity values and ICD decisions (71% concordant vs. 29%, p = .06)' but this is about concordance, not knowledge. source_quote: DA participants reported improved knowledge relative to UC (B=.41, p<.05)
reasoning: Continuous outcome: Knowledge. The text reports a regression coefficient B=0.41 for improved knowledge in DA vs UC, but not raw means. Cochrane raw_data shows exp_mean=64.4, SD=18.5, n=196 vs ctrl_mean=61.7, SD=17.8, n=185. MD = 64.4 - 61.7 = 2.7, matching Cochrane effect. Using Cochrane data.

source_quote: realistic expectations (risk of 'hypo', 'weight gain', 'complications'; 81.0% vs 5.2%, 70.5% vs 5.3%, 26.3% vs 5.0% respectively, p<0.001)
reasoning: Binary outcome: Accurate risk perceptions. Text reports realistic expectations for various risks. For 'hypo' risk: intervention 81.0% vs control 5.2%. Using raw counts from Cochrane: exp_cases=67, exp_n=95 vs ctrl_cases=4, ctrl_n=75. These correspond to 67/95=70.5% vs 4/75=5.3%, which matches the 'weight gain' percentage. The Cochrane data appears to represent one of the three risk perception measures (likely weight gain based on percentages).

source_quote: Informed patients were able to gauge more accurately the positive predictive value of screening (p 5 .0009).
reasoning: Binary outcome: Accurate risk perceptions. Text states 'Informed patients were able to gauge more accurately the positive predictive value of screening' with p=0.0009. Using Cochrane raw_data: exp_cases=189, exp_n=266 (71.1%) vs ctrl_cases=72, ctrl_n=133 (54.1%). These represent patients who accurately gauged risk.

source_quote: The largest reduction in decisional conflict was observed for participants in the videobooklet decision aid group (21 points).
reasoning: Continuous outcome: Decisional conflict scale (0-100). Text mentions 'The largest reduction in decisional conflict was observed for participants in the videobooklet decision aid group (21 points)' comparing videobooklet to control. Using Cochrane raw_data: exp_mean=15.9, SD=15.78, n=69 vs ctrl_mean=27.3, SD=16.61, n=69. MD = 15.9 - 27.3 = -11.4, matching Cochrane effect.

source_quote: Being offered the DA was associated with stronger treatment preferences (3.0 vs. 2.5; p=0.030) and increased subjective knowledge (6.7 vs. 6.3; p=0.022).
